metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

pyproject.toml: 28cd-d8f8-4456-482c & d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d (no ordering edge)
pyproject.toml: c9f7-d6df-bce0-4b77 & d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d (no ordering edge)
findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] At least one child or the merge-back step must verify 'pytest -m integration passes on the integrated feature worktree' to satisfy parent AC2's integration pytest tier requirement.
evidence: Parent AC2: 'The integrated feature branch passes … the default plus integration pytest tiers; verified from the feature worktree.'
125d testing: 'the full default suite pass.'
28cd testing: 'Green: focused tests pass after the three fixes. Gate: Ruff check and format-check pass across src and tests.' — no integration tier.
c9f7 testing: 'Happy path: the committed tree matches the baseline and the checker exits zero.' — no integration tier.
d9f5 testing: 'Green: focused ratchet test and full make typecheck pass.' — no integration tier.
container pairing: parent + 125d-ee6f-7af0-4200/28cd-d8f8-4456-482c/c9f7-d6df-bce0-4b77/d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d
findings.finding: No child story mentions the integration pytest tier, yet the parent AC2 requires the integrated branch to pass both 'the default plus integration pytest tiers'; all children refer only to the default suite, leaving the integration tier uncovered with no remainder owner.
scenarios: The children are completed, all their ACs pass, but the integration test suite is never run against the feature branch; a regression in time-sensitive or network-touching code (e.g., the DTZ fix altering timezone semantics) is caught only post-merge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to one child's acceptance criteria (28cd is natural since it has behavior-adjacent semantic changes to datetime code): 'Focused integration tests (if any) for validation timestamps and security-rule freshness pass; verified by pytest -m integration from the feature worktree.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define explicit sequencing or file-level coordination between 125d and 28cd for edits to tests/unit/test_ruff_ratchet.py and pyproject.toml [tool.ruff.lint] select, so neither story's assertions clobber the other's.
evidence: 125d scope: 'tests/unit/test_ruff_ratchet.py'
28cd scope: 'tests/unit/test_ruff_ratchet.py if a configuration contract test is needed'
Both children also modify pyproject.toml [tool.ruff.lint] select.
container pairing: parent + 125d-ee6f-7af0-4200/28cd-d8f8-4456-482c/c9f7-d6df-bce0-4b77/d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d
findings.finding: Children 125d (Ruff RUF) and 28cd (Ruff ASYNC/DTZ) both list tests/unit/test_ruff_ratchet.py in their scope without any explicit coordination or sequencing constraint, creating a scope overlap that can produce merge conflicts or broken test assertions if developed in parallel.
scenarios: If 28cd's patchset and 125d's patchset are rebased in the feature branch, the test file edits conflict or one story's assertions overwrite the other's, breaking a gate that was passing before the rebase.

If 28cd lands first and adds a test asserting ASYNC+DTZ are selected, then 125d modifies the same test function without accounting for 28cd's assertions, the RUF assertion is correct but the ASYNC/DTZ assertion is silently dropped.
findings.suggested_fix: Either sequence the stories explicitly (e.g., 28cd must land before 125d so 125d's test asserts on the final combined select list), or split the contract tests into separate test functions in the same file with each child owning a named function.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the specific RUF rule codes (beyond RUF100) that are in scope for the Ruff RUF story, or state the default ('enable all non-ambiguous RUF rules except RUF001, RUF002, RUF003') so the executor can act without clarification.
evidence: 'clear RUF100 and the approved non-ambiguous RUF findings, while explicitly deferring RUF001, RUF002, and RUF003'
ABSENCE: no enumeration of the specific RUF rule codes beyond RUF100 that are in scope
findings.finding: The Ruff RUF story scope refers to 'approved non-ambiguous RUF findings' without enumerating which specific RUF rule codes (beyond RUF100) are included, leaving the exact rule set undefined and requiring the executor to make a judgment call about what 'approved non-ambiguous' means in practice.
scenarios: An executor running the story cannot determine without external context which RUF rules (e.g. RUF010, RUF012, RUF013…) are 'approved' vs deferred, risking either under-enabling or over-enabling rules.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'approved non-ambiguous RUF findings' with an explicit list of rule codes, e.g. 'enable RUF100 and RUF010–RUF019 (excluding RUF001, RUF002, RUF003); defer all others pending audit'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief description of `config-check` to the plan body (what it checks, which Make target, which story wires it in) so AC2's verification step is grounded in described work.
evidence: AC2 names 'config-check' as a required passing gate
Success Criteria section names only 'make lint and make typecheck entrypoints' — no mention of config-check
Neither Context, Approach, nor Child Decomposition describes config-check as a task, target, or gate to be wired up
ABSENCE: no plan section explains what config-check is, who owns it, or how it relates to the four child stories
findings.finding: 'config-check' appears in AC2 as a required passing gate but is never described anywhere in the plan body (Context, Approach, Decomposition, or Success Criteria), making it an orphan verification step with no described deliverable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2: 'The integrated feature branch passes make lint, make typecheck, config-check, and the default plus integration pytest tiers'
scenarios: An implementer completing the four stories and integration verification has no described work to satisfy the config-check gate, yet AC2 blocks closure on it
If config-check is a new target that must be created or wired, it is undescribed work with no child story
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach or Success Criteria section, add one sentence such as: 'config-check (make config-check) validates tool configuration files and is run as part of the integration verification step; no new story is required if it already passes on the feature branch.' Then confirm whether it is a pre-existing target or one to be created.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out plan for each gate (e.g., a named revert-commit procedure or a documented config escape hatch) so that if a gate causes unexpected breakage post-merge, there is a stated, bounded recovery path.
evidence: 'Deliver four independently reviewed stories into a short-lived Gerrit feature branch, then merge the completed branch atomically to main.'
AC: 'The final no-ff merge is submitted and replicated to origin/main; verified by fetching origin and confirming every child rebar-ticket commit is reachable from origin/main.'

ABSENCE: No revert/rollback procedure is stated for the post-merge state — e.g., no mention of a revert commit strategy, a gate-loosening escape hatch, or a documented procedure to temporarily disable a gate if it fires unexpectedly on main.
findings.finding: The plan describes an atomic no-ff merge to main as a one-way operation but provides no explicit back-out plan if the newly enforced gates cause unexpected breakage after merge (e.g., a dependent tool or CI environment reacts differently post-merge than on the feature branch).
findings.location: Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria — 'The final no-ff merge is submitted and replicated to origin/main'
scenarios: A newly merged gate fires on a legitimate code path that was not present on the feature branch (e.g., a dependency update lands simultaneously), blocking all further merges to main with no documented recovery path.
An edge case in the C901 shrink-only baseline causes false positives in a CI environment that differs from the feature worktree, with no stated procedure to loosen the gate temporarily.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection under Approach: 'If a gate is found to misfire after merge, the on-call engineer will submit a revert of the relevant story commit (Gerrit change-id documented on the child ticket) or temporarily widen the threshold via a fast-track config patch, subject to the same LLM-Review +1 gate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the C901 per-symbol baseline and threshold value (15) are stored in a named, version-controlled config file (e.g., `pyproject.toml` `[tool.flake8]` / `.flake8`) rather than inlined into a Makefile or CI script, so future threshold reductions are discoverable and consistent.
evidence: 'add a symbol-level shrink-only C901 baseline at threshold 15 and wire it into the canonical lint gate'
Plan is silent on *where* the baseline values are stored — no config-file target is named
'shrink-only' implies the threshold list will be updated repeatedly as debt is paid; discoverability of the storage location matters
findings.finding: The C901 complexity threshold of 15 and the 'shrink-only baseline' mechanism are thresholds expected to evolve downward over time, but the plan does not specify that these values will be stored in a version-controlled, human-editable config file (e.g., pyproject.toml, .flake8, setup.cfg) rather than inlined into a Makefile rule or CI script where they would be harder to discover and update.
findings.location: Child Decomposition — Complexity story: 'add a symbol-level shrink-only C901 baseline at threshold 15'
scenarios: A future developer tries to lower a per-symbol threshold after refactoring and cannot find where it is defined because it was inlined in a Makefile target
A new contributor sees an opaque lint failure with no obvious config file to consult
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the Complexity story description: 'The per-symbol baseline and the threshold value 15 are recorded in `.flake8` (or `pyproject.toml` `[tool.flake8]`) so that reducing a symbol's threshold is a one-line config edit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan requirement to document the shrink-only C901 baseline pattern in AGENTS.md or an ADR, covering: what tool/mechanism enforces the shrink-only constraint, where the baseline file lives, and how a developer is expected to update it.
evidence: 'add a symbol-level shrink-only C901 baseline at threshold 15 and wire it into the canonical lint gate'
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or any design document update anywhere in the plan
A 'shrink-only' per-symbol baseline is a non-standard extension of standard C901 enforcement and constitutes a novel architectural/tooling decision
findings.finding: The 'shrink-only C901 baseline' pattern is a novel tooling contract — it introduces a per-symbol complexity ratchet mechanism that is not standard flake8/C901 behavior and which future maintainers will need to understand. The plan mentions no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc entry to capture this decision.
findings.location: Child Decomposition — Complexity story; Success Criteria
scenarios: A new maintainer adds a new function, sees a C901 failure, and does not understand the shrink-only contract — they may raise the baseline instead of refactoring
The mechanism for enforcing 'shrink-only' (e.g., a custom script, a sorted baseline file diff) is undiscoverable without documentation

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the C901 shrink-only implementation mechanism: specify whether per-file-ignores, per-function inline `# noqa: C901` comments, or a test-enforced allowlist will carry the existing 102 hits, and how the 'shrink-only' invariant is enforced (e.g., a test analogous to `test_mypy_ratchet.py` that fails if the exemption set grows).
evidence: Plan text: 'add a symbol-level shrink-only C901 baseline at threshold 15 and wire it into the canonical lint gate'
pyproject.toml [tool.ruff.lint] select does not include C90/C901; no [tool.ruff.lint.mccabe] section exists
docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md:119: '102 C901 hits' at the existing advisory threshold
The existing shrink-only pattern (per-file-ignores for BLE001/T201) is file-level, not symbol-level — ruff has no per-function allowlist equivalent
findings.finding: The plan describes the C901 baseline as 'symbol-level shrink-only' but Ruff has no native per-symbol/per-function allowlist primitive — its only enforcement scoping mechanisms are per-file-ignores (file-granularity) and inline `# noqa: C901` comments (manually maintained). With 102 existing C901 hits at threshold 15, the implementation mechanism (per-file-ignores vs. inline noqas vs. a test-enforced shrink list) is unspecified, and each choice has different blast-radius and shrink-only enforcement characteristics. This leaves a mechanism gap the child story must resolve by convention or tooling that the plan doesn't specify.
findings.location: Child Decomposition — Complexity story: 'add a symbol-level shrink-only C901 baseline at threshold 15'
scenarios: If the story uses per-file-ignores, every function in an exempted file is silently allowed regardless of complexity, making 'symbol-level' a misnomer and allowing new over-complex functions in exempted files without a gate failure.
If the story uses inline `# noqa: C901`, the 'shrink-only' guarantee requires an external enforcer (a test or a separate grep-based ratchet) that the plan doesn't mention.
findings.suggested_fix: Model the C901 ratchet on the existing module-size-allowlist strangler: enumerate over-threshold functions in a committed shrink-only list file (e.g., `.github/c901-allowlist.txt`) and add a CI step (or a pytest test) that fails if any function NOT in the allowlist exceeds the threshold, and fails if the allowlist grows beyond its current size.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale sentence to the Approach section explaining why four independently scoped stories on a feature branch fits this particular problem (e.g., each story's gate scope is cleanly bounded by one tool, regressions are isolatable per story, and the feature-branch integration checkpoint catches cross-story interactions such as warn_unused_ignores exposing stale noqas before touching main).
evidence: Plan text states only the negative case: 'Rejected: one large cleanup change, because it would mix distinct tools, obscure regressions, and make rollback difficult.'

No affirmative statement of why four stories + feature branch fits this problem on the positive dimensions: independent regression isolation, per-story gate verifiability, cross-story interaction detection at the feature-branch integration point, etc.
Rubric criterion G6 explicitly states: 'The plan states a POSITIVE rationale for the chosen approach (its absence is a finding); do NOT require a rejected-alternatives section (anti-priming).'
findings.finding: The plan's Approach section states only WHY the rejected alternative (one large cleanup change) is bad, but does not affirmatively state WHY the chosen approach — four independently reviewed stories delivered via a short-lived feature branch — is the right fit for this problem. The rubric requires a positive rationale for the chosen approach; its absence is a finding.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Rejected: one large cleanup change, because it would mix distinct tools, obscure regressions, and make rollback difficult.'
scenarios: An implementer reading only the Approach section understands what NOT to do but not what positive properties the chosen approach delivers, making it harder to make consistent sub-decisions within each story.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for the C901 'shrink-only baseline': describe whether existing violations are cleared (refactored down to ≤15), annotated with `# noqa: C901` under a test-enforced count cap, or tracked via a separate baseline file — and update the Complexity child story accordingly.
evidence: docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md line 119: '102 `C901` hits' at the current codebase baseline
pyproject.toml [tool.ruff.lint] select = ["E", "F", "W", "I", "UP", "B", "BLE001", "T201", "SIM115"] — C901 is absent; no [tool.ruff.lint.mccabe] stanza exists in pyproject.toml
Ruff's C901 (McCabe complexity) applies a global max-complexity threshold per-function — there is no native 'allowlist of known-exceeding symbols' configuration surface in ruff
ABSENCE: plan names no supplemental mechanism (test harness, noqa annotation strategy, or baseline file) to operationalize 'shrink-only' enforcement for 102 existing violations
Evidence class: Partially-verified — ruff C901 capability exists but the 'shrink-only baseline' enforcement mechanism is UNVERIFIED as described
findings.finding: The plan asserts a 'symbol-level shrink-only C901 baseline' but ruff's C901 support (via [tool.ruff.lint.mccabe] max-complexity) operates as a global threshold with no native per-symbol allowlist or shrink-only enforcement mechanism. With 102 existing C901 hits at threshold 15 (per the maintenance audit), simply enabling C901 in ruff's select at threshold 15 would cause 102 immediate failures. The plan does not describe the concrete implementation mechanism (e.g., per-function `# noqa: C901` annotations with a supplemental test asserting the count never grows, or a separate baseline file) by which 'shrink-only' enforcement is achieved.
findings.location: Child Decomposition — Complexity story: 'add a symbol-level shrink-only C901 baseline at threshold 15 and wire it into the canonical lint gate'
scenarios: An implementer adding `C901` to ruff's select at max-complexity=15 immediately produces 102 failures and cannot ship the story without either (a) clearing all 102 functions or (b) adding per-function noqa annotations — neither of which is described as the plan's approach
If noqa annotations are used, a 'shrink-only' guarantee requires a separate test that counts or enumerates them — without that test, a developer can simply add a new noqa and the count grows silently
findings.suggested_fix: Choose one of: (a) clear all 102 existing violations by refactoring functions over complexity 15 before enabling C901 (appropriate if the scope is bounded — audit the worst offenders from the runbook's list); (b) add `# noqa: C901` to each of the 102 sites and add a test that asserts `len(noqa_c901_sites) <= N` (shrink-only via test); (c) use ruff's `per-file-ignores` for files with known-exceeding functions combined with a CI-enforced allowlist test. Document the chosen approach in the child story's acceptance criteria.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define or cite the 'LLM-Review' Gerrit label vocabulary (permissible vote values), identify which agent/service casts the vote, specify the rubric or prompt it applies, and add a fallback procedure for when it is unavailable or returns a non-+1 result.
evidence: Plan quotes 'LLM-Review +1' as a required gate but never defines: (a) the vote-label vocabulary (e.g. -2/-1/0/+1/+2), (b) what scoring rubric or prompt the LLM reviewer applies, (c) which agent/service provides the review, or (d) what happens if the LLM reviewer is unavailable or returns a non-+1 verdict (fallback for failed sub-step).
No referenced agent spec, prompt, or enum definition was found in the plan text for 'LLM-Review'.
findings.finding: The plan references an 'LLM-Review +1' Gerrit vote label in the operator-attested AC without defining or citing the schema/enum of vote values, the decision protocol the LLM reviewer uses, or where that reviewer is specified — making the referenced agent/skill unverifiable and its approval semantics undefined within the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — '[operator-attested] Every story change and the final merge-back receive LLM-Review +1 and Verified +1 with no unresolved comments in Gerrit.'
scenarios: A story author submits a change and cannot determine whether 'LLM-Review +1' is satisfied by a score of 0 vs +1 vs +2, or which tool to invoke to obtain it.
If the LLM reviewer returns a negative score or is unavailable, no fallback or escalation path is described, leaving the Gerrit gate in an undefined state.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'LLM Reviewer' subsection (or link to an existing spec) that states: the Gerrit label name and valid vote range (e.g. -1/0/+1), the tool or workflow that triggers the review, the evaluation rubric (e.g. link to the rebar review prompt), and the fallback action if the reviewer is unavailable (e.g. require two human Verified +1 votes instead).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] At least one child (or the merge-back story) must list 'make config-check passes on the integrated feature worktree' in its verification commands or acceptance criteria to satisfy parent AC2.
evidence: Parent AC2: 'The integrated feature branch passes make lint, make typecheck, config-check, and the default plus integration pytest tiers; verified from the feature worktree.'
No child (125d, 28cd, c9f7, d9f5) lists 'config-check' in its scope, testing section, or acceptance criteria.
The config-check gate is a named CI step in .github/workflows/_build-and-test.yml line 109-110 ('make config-check').
container pairing: parent + 125d-ee6f-7af0-4200/28cd-d8f8-4456-482c/c9f7-d6df-bce0-4b77/d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d
findings.finding: No child story includes 'config-check' in its verification scope or commands, yet the parent AC2 explicitly requires the integrated feature branch to pass 'make config-check' as a named gate alongside make lint and make typecheck.
scenarios: An implementer completes all four children's ACs and merges the feature branch without running make config-check on the integrated worktree, leaving the parent AC2 unverifiable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a verification step to the merge-back or to one child (e.g., c9f7 since it already owns Makefile): 'make config-check passes on the integrated feature worktree; verified from the feature worktree after all four children are merged.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Child 28cd must specify whether the validate_checks.py DTZ fix uses datetime.timezone.utc (py310-compatible, UTC semantics) or date.today() local-date semantics, and must add a focused test that pins the timezone-awareness choice and its effect on hour-cluster grouping.
evidence: pyproject.toml line 271-273: 'target-version is intentionally kept at the conservative py310 floor (not py311): it keeps the gating UP set from pushing 3.11-only idioms (e.g. datetime.UTC)'
validate_checks.py line 65: 'ts = str(datetime.fromtimestamp(created))[:19]' — timezone-naive, DTZ011 finding
28cd approach: 'fix the three DTZ sites using explicit timezone-aware or local-date semantics' — does not specify datetime.timezone.utc vs datetime.UTC
container pairing: parent + 125d-ee6f-7af0-4200/28cd-d8f8-4456-482c/c9f7-d6df-bce0-4b77/d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d
findings.finding: Child 28cd's DTZ fix to validate_checks.py uses datetime.fromtimestamp (currently timezone-naive, line 65) but the pyproject.toml comment explicitly documents that target-version=py310 was chosen to prevent pushing datetime.UTC (a 3.11-only idiom); if the implementer uses datetime.UTC to fix the DTZ finding, this conflicts with the established py310 target constraint. Additionally, the semantic choice between UTC and local-time for fromtimestamp affects the hour-clustering behavior.
scenarios: An implementer uses datetime.UTC to satisfy DTZ011; ruff UP does not flag it at py310 target, but the code now contains a 3.11-only attribute that the project's own config comment declared off-limits.
An implementer changes fromtimestamp to UTC-aware, shifting the hour-clustering grouping key from local-wall-clock to UTC, silently changing behavior for users in non-UTC timezones.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend 28cd's approach to state: 'Use datetime.timezone.utc (not datetime.UTC — see pyproject.toml target-version rationale) for the fromtimestamp fix; add a test asserting the fix produces UTC-aware datetimes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the required landing order of 125d and 28cd in the feature branch (28cd must land before 125d, or the final integrated lint gate must re-verify RUF100 after both stories are applied) to prevent a stale-noqa regression at integration.
evidence: 125d AC2: 'RUF100 reports no stale noqa comments under the full configured rule set'
28cd approach: 'fix the three DTZ sites using explicit timezone-aware or local-date semantics' — approach does not address whether inline noqa comments will be used
28cd AC2: 'All three existing DTZ findings are fixed without blanket per-file ignores' — does not exclude targeted inline noqa comments
container pairing: parent + 125d-ee6f-7af0-4200/28cd-d8f8-4456-482c/c9f7-d6df-bce0-4b77/d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d
findings.finding: Children 125d (RUF, which enables RUF100 stale-noqa detection) and 28cd (ASYNC/DTZ, which may add inline noqa comments as part of its fixes) have an implicit ordering dependency: if 125d lands before 28cd, it will clear RUF100 violations under the rule set that excludes ASYNC/DTZ; any noqa comments added by 28cd's fixes would then not be re-checked by RUF100 in the same pass. Conversely, if 28cd lands first and its fixes introduce any inline noqa comments, 125d's RUF100 sweep must re-check them.
scenarios: 28cd lands first and adds a targeted `# noqa: DTZ011` on a line it cannot fully fix; 125d then runs RUF100 over the full selected set (which now includes DTZ) and correctly flags that noqa as stale if the underlying fix was made, but the stale comment was never cleaned.

125d lands first, clears all noqa comments under {E,F,W,I,UP,B,BLE001,T201,SIM115,RUF}, then 28cd adds ASYNC+DTZ and introduces new noqa comments that RUF100 would flag — but no pass re-runs RUF100 after 28cd.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 125d's acceptance criteria: 'RUF100 is verified clean after all sibling Ruff stories (28cd) have landed in the feature branch, not only after this story's isolated change.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
